name: Sorting & Classifying Challenge
description: Learners sort objects by single properties (color, size, shape), then by multiple properties simultaneously (red AND large). Develops classification thinking and the foundation for understanding logical AND/OR. Uses household items: buttons, shells, blocks, or pictures.
materials: Buttons, shells, blocks, or printed pictures
learning_objectives: Sort objects systematically by one property
Sort objects by multiple properties simultaneously
Understand the logic of inclusion and exclusion
Use language to describe classification rules
variations: Use AND logic: 'Show me all the things that are red AND round'
Use OR logic: 'Show me all the things that are red OR square'
Create your own property: 'Sort by your own rule, then explain it to a partner'
Difficulty levels: start with obvious properties (color) then abstract (rough vs. smooth)

name: If-Then Chain Reaction Game
description: Learners create a chain of if-then statements and act them out. 'If I clap, then you jump. If you jump, then I spin. If I spin, then we all laugh.' Builds understanding of cause-and-effect and logical consequences in a playful, embodied way.
materials: No materials required (or: paper and markers to write chain statements)
learning_objectives: Understand if-then relationships in real contexts
Create logical chains of cause-and-effect
Recognize consequences and predict outcomes
Use if-then language naturally
variations: Nature theme: 'If it rains, then the ground gets wet. If the ground gets wet, then plants grow.'
Story theme: Use a favorite story and map out all the if-then connections
Debate version: 'If we go to the park, then we might get tired. If we get tired, then we need a snack.'
Written version: Learners write if-then statements instead of acting them out

name: Pattern Recognition & Prediction
description: Learners identify repeating patterns (visual, number, sound, movement) and predict what comes next. Patterns start simple (A-B-A-B) and grow complex (A-B-B-A-B-B-A-B-B). Develops logical thinking and the foundational skill of recognizing structure.
learning_objectives: Identify simple repeating patterns
Recognize more complex pattern structures
Predict the next element with confidence
Create and explain patterns to others
variations: Visual patterns: colors, shapes, sizes
Number patterns: 2, 4, 6, 8... or Fibonacci-like sequences
Sound patterns: clap, snap, clap, snap...
Movement patterns: jump, spin, jump, spin...
Create-your-own: Learners make a pattern and have peers guess the rule

name: Argument Builder Card Game
description: Card game where learners build arguments by combining claim cards with reason cards. Each valid combination earns points. Teaches structure of arguments: a claim needs reasons, and some combinations work better than others. Playful introduction to argument validity.
learning_objectives: Identify what makes a claim and a reason
Understand that claims need supporting reasons
Evaluate whether a reason actually supports a claim
Construct clear, logical arguments
variations: Beginner: Obvious matches (strong reasons with matching claims)
Intermediate: Some weak reasons that don't quite fit
Advanced: Reason cards that are true but irrelevant
Debate version: Two teams argue about whether a match is valid

name: Valid or Invalid? Reasoning Courtroom
description: Learners act out a courtroom where arguments are 'tried' for validity. Prosecution presents an argument, defense attacks its reasoning, judge decides: valid or invalid, sound or unsound. Makes argument evaluation dramatic and memorable.
learning_objectives: Distinguish valid from invalid argument structure
Distinguish sound from unsound (true premises)
Critique arguments constructively
Defend reasoning under pressure
variations: Simple version: Only valid vs. invalid (structure)
Advanced: Also evaluate soundness (true/false premises)
Real-world version: Use actual arguments from news, politics, or advertising
Appeal process: Loser can appeal and argue for reconsideration

name: Fallacy Detective: Ad Campaign Analysis
description: Learners analyze print and video advertisements, identifying fallacies and manipulation tactics. They name the fallacy (ad hominem, hasty generalization, appeal to authority, etc.), explain how it fails, and redesign the ad with valid reasoning.
materials: Drawing or presentation materials for redesigns
learning_objectives: Identify fallacies in real advertisements
Name the fallacy correctly
Explain why the fallacy is misleading
Construct more honest, logical advertising
variations: Detective game: Learners award 'fallacy points' to ads with most/most obvious fallacies
Redesign challenge: Fix the ad's logic while keeping the product appealing
Cross-media: Analyze ads, political speeches, social media posts, news headlines
Create your own bad ad: Students intentionally create ads with fallacies for peers to identify

name: Propaganda Poster Deconstruction
description: Learners analyze historical and contemporary propaganda posters, identifying persuasion techniques (loaded language, false dilemmas, appeals to fear or authority). They deconstruct how the poster manipulates emotion instead of using logic, then create their own counter-poster with logical arguments.
learning_objectives: Identify propaganda techniques in visual and textual rhetoric
Analyze how persuasion works through emotion vs. logic
Distinguish emotional appeals from logical arguments
Create persuasive messages using evidence and reason instead of manipulation
variations: Historical: Compare propaganda from WWII, Cold War, different countries
Contemporary: Analyze modern political posters, PSAs, corporate messaging
Create counter-propaganda: Design a logical response to a manipulative message
Media literacy: Extend to news headlines, memes, and social media

name: Truth Table Construction Workshop
description: Learners construct complete truth tables for compound propositions (p ∧ q, p ∨ q, p → q, etc.). They discover logical equivalences (De Morgan's Laws, contrapositive) by comparing truth tables. Uses visual layouts and interactive tools to build fluency with symbolic logic.
materials: Computer or tablet (optional, for interactive tools)
learning_objectives: Translate English propositions to symbolic form
Construct accurate truth tables with correct number of rows
Evaluate compound propositions for all truth value combinations
Identify logical equivalences by comparing tables
variations: Beginner: Simple two-variable propositions (p ∧ q, p ∨ q)
Intermediate: Three-variable propositions and longer chains
Advanced: Discover equivalences and prove them with truth tables
Interactive: Use online truth table generators to experiment and verify

name: Logic Gate Circuit Builder
description: Learners design logic circuits using AND, OR, and NOT gates. They learn that circuit behavior mirrors truth tables: gates represent logical operations, inputs map to proposition truth values, outputs to conclusions. Bridges symbolic logic with physical/computational systems.
materials: Logic gate simulation software (free tools available)
learning_objectives: Understand that logic gates implement logical operations
Design circuits to solve logical problems
Translate between logical propositions and circuit diagrams
Debug circuit logic
variations: Simulation-based: Use software to experiment without physical hardware
Hardware-based: Build actual circuits with breadboards, LEDs, batteries
Problem-based: 'Design a circuit that outputs true only when inputs match'
Computer science connection: Relate to Boolean circuits in computer architecture

name: Proof by Contradiction Puzzle
description: Learners solve logic puzzles using proof by contradiction. For example: 'Three friends go to dinner. Exactly one ordered fish. Alex says Chen ordered fish. Ben says Alex ordered fish. Chen says Alex didn't order fish. Exactly two are lying. Who ordered fish?' Learners assume each possibility, derive contradictions, find the truth.
learning_objectives: Understand proof by contradiction as a technique
Assume a claim and systematically derive consequences
Recognize when a contradiction emerges
Conclude the opposite of the false assumption
variations: Beginner: Simple puzzles with few variables
Intermediate: Multi-person logic grid puzzles
Advanced: Mathematical proofs using contradiction (square root of 2 is irrational, etc.)
Create your own: Learners design puzzles for peers to solve

name: Mathematical Induction Domino Challenge
description: Learners set up dominoes in a line, knock over the first one, and watch the chain reaction. They see induction in action: if one domino falls, it knocks over the next; if we prove the first case and the chain rule, all dominoes fall. Then they prove actual mathematical induction proofs.
materials: Whiteboard or paper for writing proofs
Examples of induction proofs (sum formulas, inequalities, etc.)
learning_objectives: Understand mathematical induction as a proof technique
Prove a base case correctly
Prove the inductive step (if true for n, then true for n+1)
Recognize when induction applies
variations: Physical demo: Dominoes or chain reaction with objects
Formula proofs: Sum of integers, sum of squares, factorial inequalities
Sequence proofs: Prove properties of Fibonacci, geometric sequences
Algorithm proofs: Prove algorithm correctness or loop invariants

name: Proof Construction Workshop
description: Guided workshop where learners write their first formal proofs. Starting with very simple direct proofs (if-then statements with 2–3 steps), they progress to longer arguments, conditional statements, and proof by contradiction. Emphasis on clarity and logical flow, not cleverness.
materials: List of statements to prove (geometric, algebraic, or logical)
Reference sheet of logical inference rules
learning_objectives: Write clear, logically valid proofs
Use correct mathematical and logical notation
Justify each step with a reason
Distinguish different proof techniques and when to use each
variations: Beginner: Guided proofs with blank steps to fill in
Intermediate: Partially written proofs to complete
Advanced: Prove statements from scratch
Collaborative: Peer review and revision of proofs
Real-world: Prove statements relevant to learners' math/CS courses
